ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for eggnog exists; A0FKN (milk-based flavoured beverages) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for packaged eggnog beverage; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of packaged eggnog is approximately one cup (240 ml / ~254 g), as commonly consumed during festive occasions.

nutrient_source.title: Eggnog — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170875)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced from the HPB Singapore database (confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for eggnog (FDC ID 170875). Saturated fat, cholesterol, calcium, potassium, and micronutrient values are drawn from USDA SR Legacy. Minor rounding differences exist between HPB and USDA values; HPB values are used as primary for macros per source priority rules.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for packaged eggnog was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated using sweetened whole-milk dairy beverage analogues (flavoured milk GI ~60–70, mean ~65) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (38.1 g from 254 g serving × 15 g/100 g) / 100 = 24.7.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (14.16 g/100 g) in a beverage exceeds the 5 g/100 g sugar threshold for beverages; insulin-driven sodium retention and weight gain independently elevate blood pressure risk.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid regular consumption of packaged eggnog; the high sugar load (14.16 g/100 g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain, independently raising blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, even though sodium content is relatively low at 71 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (14.16 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre in a liquid form; rapid glucose absorption causes significant postprandial glycaemic spikes; GI estimated at 65 (medium-high for a beverage).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit packaged eggnog; the combination of 14.16 g sugar/100 g, zero dietary fibre, and liquid delivery results in rapid glucose absorption and significant postprandial blood glucose elevation, making this a high-concern sugar-sweetened beverage.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.8 g/100 g from cream) combined with high sugar (14.16 g/100 g) and cholesterol (59 mg/100 g) contributes to atherogenic lipid profiles and endothelial dysfunction.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of packaged eggnog; the saturated fat content (~4.8 g/100 g), combined with high sugar and dietary cholesterol (59 mg/100 g), promotes LDL elevation and endothelial dysfunction, increasing cardiovascular risk per ACC/AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar (14.16 g/100 g) and saturated fat (~4.8 g/100 g) promote pro-inflammatory mediators; zero dietary fibre provides no anti-inflammatory benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid packaged eggnog; the high sugar and saturated fat content promote systemic inflammation, and the absence of dietary fibre or anti-inflammatory components provides no mitigating benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged eggnog is a ready-to-drink, commercially processed sweetened dairy beverage made from whole milk, cream, eggs, sugar, and spices such as nutmeg and vanilla. It has a rich, creamy texture with a sweet, custard-like flavour and a characteristic warm spice aroma. Traditionally associated with Christmas and winter holiday celebrations in North America and the United Kingdom, packaged eggnog is sold pre-pasteurised and ready to consume without preparation. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 150 kcal, 4.16 g protein, 8.33 g fat (of which ~4.8 g is saturated), 15 g carbohydrate, and 14.16 g sugar with zero dietary fibre. The high sugar content (14.16 g/100 g) and liquid delivery format make it a high-glycaemic-load beverage with an estimated GI of 65. Clinically, it is a high-concern food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis due to its sugar-sweetened beverage classification, saturated fat content, and absence of fibre. Calcium content (~130 mg/100 g) and vitamin B12 (~0.4 mcg/100 g) are notable positive micronutrients from the dairy base. It is not suitable for vegans, and halal status depends on the specific brand and egg sourcing.
common_user_queries: Is eggnog good for high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of eggnog?
How much fat is in eggnog?
Can I drink eggnog if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore database at 0.95 confidence and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170875). Micronutrient values drawn from USDA SR Legacy. GI estimated from peer-reviewed analogous dairy beverage data (Atkinson et al. 2008). All values are internally consistent and supported by approved sources.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source; eggnog is a refined dairy-sugar beverage with no plant fibre components. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace per USDA SR Legacy for standard commercial eggnog). calcium_mg value of 130 mg/100 g is sourced from USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 170875); the input record reported 0.0 for calcium which was an error in the source input — corrected to 130 mg/100 g per USDA. iron_mg of 0.09 mg/100 g sourced from USDA SR Legacy; input reported 0.0 which was corrected. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.41), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.37), cholesterol_mg (59.0), potassium_mg (165.0), and all micronutrient values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170875. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened flavoured milk beverages per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct eggnog GI measurement was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Halal status is brand-dependent and not confirmed for generic packaged eggnog. The input dietary_tags 'low_calorie' is disputed: at 150 kcal/100 g and 254 g per serving (~381 kcal/serving), this is not a low-calorie beverage; this tag has been removed from the output classification.
